brief_justification: Correctly answers Yes and precisely identifies Shannon as the domain-free generalization of which Boltzmann is a concrete instantiation; Goes three levels deep — domain-bound vs. domain-free distinction, set-subset mathematical relationship, and separate treatment of the philosopher's gloss as categorically different; Shows some process awareness ('more precise than just higher abstraction') but does not explicitly name biases or course-correct mid-analysis; Implicitly verifies via the subset argument ('every valid application of Boltzmann is also an application of Shannon, but not vice versa') without running explicit counterfactuals or boundary tests; Presents the Boltzmann-as-uniform-distribution claim with full confidence, omitting that this is a simplification (canonical ensemble vs. microcanonical), and states no explicit confidence levels; Systematically addresses the philosopher's description as a third category and explains why it is different in kind rather than higher or lower, with specific reasons; Every conclusion traces to an explicit derivation — the domain-free/domain-bound distinction, the mathematical instantiation claim, and the hierarchy are all shown step by step such that a third party could reproduce the chain.
